parsererror.style: display: block; white-space: pre; border: 2px solid #c77; padding: 0 1em 0 1em; margin: 1em; background-color: #fdd; color: black
parsererror.h3: This page contains the following errors:
parsererror.div: error on line 957 at column 342: Invalid bytes in character encoding
parsererror.h3: Below is a rendering of the page up to the first error.

channel.title: School
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian

item.title: CheatSheet S
item.description:
#postmodern #novel-within-novel

JJ Abrams, Doug Dorst, S (2013)
genre: #postmodern #novel-within-novel


mystery
novel-within-novel
comments on modern world:
chat in margins
e-book...?


first question: what kind of a book is this?!
in what way does it reflect on our time and culture?
book as a physical object, not just the text (authors’ intention) → does it work?
it goes on and on and on online, including official websites, such as the Eot-vos Wheel, the Jen Blog, etc.
is the solving of the mysteries presented in the novel relevant? In what way?


novel:
S.
Sola
Stenfalk (leader of a group of resistance fighters(?))
Corbeau (S.’s lover)
Ostrero
Pfeifer
Vévoda (opponent, dictator(?))
readers:
Jen
Eric
authorship and mystery
Straka
FXC (Filomena X. Caldera, translator)
Durand
Desjardin
Ekstrom
Signe Rabe
Summersby
Moody (Eric’s former professor)
Ilsa (Moody’s assistant, Eric’s rival)


monkey
birds
ships


Pencil: Eric wrote in pencil while taking notes during his early reading(s) of Ship of Theseus before he met Jen.
Blue (Jen) and black (Eric): This is the first pass of notes between Eric and Jen after they “meet” in the margins.
Green (Eric) and orange (Jen): This is the second pass of comments between Eric and Jen after their relationship has deepened.
Purple (Jen) and red (Eric): This is the third set of comments between the two, after they have met in person.
Black (both Jen and Eric): These are the final set of notes and include their comments after the move to Prague.

S.(amnesiac protagonistof Ship of Theseus)Sola(woman S. pursues)Vévoda(arms dealer antagonist)Maelstrom(ship captain)Jennifer Heyward(Jen, graduate studentstudying Straka)Eric Husch(undergraduate obsessedwith Straka mystery)V.M. Straka(mysterious author ofShip of Theseus)F.X. Caldeira(Straka's translatorand collaborator)Dr. Moody(Jen's professor)Desjardins(Straka scholar) pursues/loves opposes guidesconnected to collaborates/falls forcollaborates/falls for collaborates with student of researchesobsessed withstudies Recompile

S.(amnesiac protagonistof Ship of Theseus)Sola(woman S. pursues)Vévoda(arms dealer antagonist)Maelstrom(ship captain)Jennifer Heyward(Jen, graduate studentstudying Straka)Eric Husch(undergraduate obsessedwith Straka mystery)V.M. Straka(mysterious author ofShip of Theseus)F.X. Caldeira(Straka's translatorand collaborator)Dr. Moody(Jen's professor)Desjardins(Straka scholar)Filomela Caldeira(F.X. Caldeira's daughter)MacInnes(Straka scholar/rival)Bouchard(Durand publisher)Summersby(Straka's agent)Corbeau(mysterious figure)Pfeifer(academic administrator)Ilsa(Eric's ex-girlfriend) pursues/loves opposes guidesconnected to collaborates/falls forcollaborates/falls for collaborates with student of researchesobsessed withstudies father to academic rivalstudies/disputespublishedrepresented mysterious connectionadministers former relationship Recompile

item.title: CheatSheet Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy
item.description:
Douglas Adams, The Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy, 1979


satire
probability and chance
bureaucracy
corruption
importance of humanity, Earth
the big questions
AI
the absurd
A future with no Earth to laugh about … dystopian or utopian?
World-building (in double sense!)


Arthur Dent
Ford Prefect
Trillian
Zaphod Beeblebrox
Slartibartfast
Why read or write science fiction? What can be illustrated with the help of science fiction?
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Adams Hitchhikers Guide to the Galaxy/CheatSheet Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy.md

item.title: CheatSheet Handmaid’s Tale
item.description:
#dystopian #feminist

Margaret Atwood, The Handmaid’s Tale, 1985
genre: #dystopian #feminist


Chapter 30, a bit further than half the novel; hard to place anyway, because of the structure of the narrative, which jumps back and forth, revealing bit by bit

mood, atmosphere?
important: Offred has very much time at hand, she can think about so many things. She goes shopping, to certain state events, and to ceremonies; later, also to the Commander in private, as well as to Nick. Apart from that: nothing to do all day
night falling: symbolism; Gilead has also fallen, not risen, onto the US
searchlight moonlight: you always have to watch your step, as well as everybody else’s. hence this moon is too bright to move secretly
Nick: later on affair btw. Offred and Nick. Nick is important. Messenger btw. Offred and Commander
mutual attraction, visible in extract: hunger
body and cover: as night covers earth, presses down, so does Offred’s clothing: as unsexual as ever possible, even protecting her from the touch of herself; hunger cannot be
sensuality: smell from the garden, heavy, rich, almost visible; tangible
one and one and one and one is not four: everybody is isolated in Gilead. alli-ances are forbidden, immoral, persecuted; Ofglen vanishes after coming too close to Offred and too much of an activist. → power
“You can’t help what you feel, but you can help how you behave.” → state wants to change that, so that feelings are repressed so much by what you are al-lowed to do that they, in effect, do change.

Chapter 23 (p. 149-150), roughly half of the novel; hard to place anyway, because of the structure of the narrative, which jumps back and forth, revealing bit by bit

mood, atmosphere?
What are the power relations? To what extent do the genders play a role in this power relationship?
What are possible symbols in the extract?
Does Offred have any power at all?
narrative: why does she say this is a reconstruction?
They are doing something forbidden: What is the moral code like in this world? How is it represented through the novel?

OffredHandmaid, narratorThe Commander (Fred)Head of householdSerena JoyWife of CommanderNickGuardian, chauffeurOfglenHandmaid, shopping partnerMoiraOffred's best friendAunt LydiaAunt at Red CenterRitaMartha (servant)CoraMartha (servant)LukeOffred's husband (pre-Gilead) Handmaid toMarried toResentful ofBreaks rules forRomantic relationshipArranges relationshipShopping partnersResistance contactBest friends (pre-Gilead)Symbol of hopeRed Center indoctrinationDiscipline at Red CenterJudgmentalKind/hopefulMarried (pre-Gilead)Separated during escape Recompile


Gilead, the society depicted in The Handmaid's Tale, is organized into a rigid and highly stratified class system, with distinct roles and uniforms for each group. Here are the principal named classes:
For Women:

Wives: The highest-ranking women, married to Commanders. They manage households and have authority over other women in the home[1][4][7].
Aunts: Older, often infertile women who train and oversee Handmaids, enforce discipline, and help maintain Gilead's ideology[1][4][7].
Handmaids: Fertile women forced to bear children for elite families. They are highly controlled and among the most oppressed[1][4][5][7].
Marthas: Domestic servants responsible for cooking and housekeeping in elite households. They have limited freedoms compared to Handmaids[4][5][7].
Econowives: Wives of lower-ranking men (such as Guardians). They perform all domestic duties and are less restricted than Handmaids or Marthas but have lower status[4][5][7].
Jezebels: Women forced into sexual slavery, often for the entertainment of elite men. They live outside the official moral code of Gilead[4][7].
Unwomen: Women who are deemed useless to Gilead (often infertile, elderly, or rebellious) and are sent to labor camps known as the Colonies[1][4][7].
Daughters: Girls born or adopted into elite families, raised to become future Wives or for arranged marriages[3].
For Men:

Commanders: The ruling elite, holding the most power in Gilead. They design and enforce the laws and social order[2][4][6].
Angels: High-ranking soldiers who fight Gilead’s wars and may be rewarded with Handmaids or Wives[4][5].
Guardians: Lower-ranking men who serve as guards, drivers, and police. They may hope for promotion to Angel or Commander[2][4][5].
Eyes: Secret police and spies tasked with rooting out dissent[4].
Economen/Econopeople: Ordinary citizens who did not commit "sins" before Gilead’s rise; they live with fewer privileges and restrictions[3][6].
Other Groups:

Sons: Boys in elite families, educated for future roles as Commanders or Angels[3].
Widows: Older women who have lost their husbands, with limited status[7].
This strict hierarchy is enforced by law, religion, and violence, with each class marked by distinctive clothing and tightly prescribed roles[1][4][7].
[1] https://the-handmaids-tale.fandom.com/wiki/Category:Social_classes
[2] https://www.reddit.com/r/TheHandmaidsTale/comments/o2xrdg/spoilers_all_gilead_social_structure_question/
[3] https://collider.com/handmaids-tale-social-stratifications-explained/
[4] https://pmt.physicsandmathstutor.com/download/English-Literature/A-level/Notes/AQA/The-Handmaid's-Tale/Themes/Class.pdf
[5] https://homework.study.com/explanation/what-is-the-hierarchy-in-gilead-as-well-as-the-functions-and-characteristics-of-each-class-group-in-the-handmaid-s-tale.html
[6] https://the-handmaids-tale.fandom.com/wiki/Republic_of_Gilead_(Series)
[7] https://screenrant.com/the-handmaids-tale-show-women-gilead-social-classes-groups-list/
[8] https://quizlet.com/gb/750579478/class-in-the-handmaids-tale-flash-cards/


mood, atmosphere of the book?
Describe the world that is created in the novel. What is especially dystopian about it?
What are the roles of men and women, respectively?
What are the power relations? To what extent do the genders play a role in this power relationship?
In what way is this a feminist novel, in what way is it not?


Misogyny
Classification
No names
Men not much better off
Religion
Denial of body, of lust, of “nature”
Oppression, isolation, brutality
Totalitarianism; in the making: memories of olden tim
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Atwood Handmaids Tale/CheatSheet Handmaid’s Tale.md

#19thcenturyfiction

Jane Austen, Lady Susan, 1794 (pub. 1871)
genre: #19thcenturyfiction


Gender
Power
Manipulation
public appearance vs. private reality
love and marriage
dependence (not least Frederica on Lady Susan, but also on the Vernons

Lady SusanMrs VernonMr VernonReginald De CourcyCatherine VernonFrederica VernonSir James MartinMrs Johnson ManipulatesManipulatesMotherEncourages MarriageConfidanteMarriedMotherAuntFatherUncleRomantic InterestCousinFriend SuitorSuitorFriend Recompile

item.title: CheatSheet Pride and Prejudice
item.description:
#19thcenturyfiction #feminist

Jane Austen, Pride and Prejudice, 1813
genre: #19thcenturyfiction #feminist

Extract 1: end of novel, chapter 50; close to everything coming to a good ending
Extract 2: ¼ of the novel, Collins has proposed to Elizabeth, she declines

characterise Elizabeth Bennet, especially starting from the extract! contrast btw. her self-conception and what else we know about her

Elizabeth BennetProtagonist, second Bennet daughterFitzwilliam DarcyWealthy landownerJane BennetEldest Bennet daughterCharles BingleyDarcy's friendLydia BennetYoungest Bennet daughterGeorge WickhamOfficerMr. BennetBennet patriarchMrs. BennetBennet matriarchMary BennetMiddle Bennet daughterKitty BennetFourth Bennet daughterCaroline BingleyBingley's sisterCharlotte LucasElizabeth's friendMr. CollinsBennet cousinLady Catherine de BourghDarcy's auntGeorgiana DarcyDarcy's sisterColonel FitzwilliamDarcy's cousinBennet MarriedFatherFatherFatherFatherFatherMotherMotherMotherMotherMotherMarriesMarriesMarriesMarriesElopes, marriesElopes, marriesSistersSistersSistersSistersSistersSistersSistersSistersSistersSistersFriendsSiblingsAims to marrySchemes againstFriendsBrotherSisterNephewAuntPatronClergymanMarriesMarriesFriendsCousin, heirRivalsAttempted elopementCousinsAcquaintances Recompile



characterise Elizabeth Bennet, especially starting from the extract! contrast btw. her self-conception and what else we know about her
What can you tell us about the Bennet family? (also starting from the extract)
daughters, parents, their relationships, nature, temperament, etc.
What can you tell us about Mr Darcy, and the development of Elizabeth’s view of him throughout the novel?
→ from there to society, its conventions, and Austen’s comment thereupon?
Are there modern/liberal/progressive figures in the novel? In what way?
characterisation in general: how is it done? how lively or stocky are the charac-ters?


famous for the irony: instance in the text?


emancipation?
family
marriage
class
men and women
pride, prejudice


What is Pride and Prejudice about?!
Is a novel like Pride and Prejudice still valid today? What can it mean to us?
Why the title?

item.title: CheatSheet Clockwork Orange
item.description:
Anthony Burgess, A Clockwork Orange, 1962

Alex (narrator; especially hates people who seem to live without a purpose; remains superficial and naïve)
Dim (stupid, big bloke, later policeman), Pete (grows up), Georgie (rebels against Alex)
Billyboy (fat, rival gang-leader, later policeman)
F. Alexander (writer, political dissident, whose wife died because she was raped by Alex’ gang)
Z. Dolin, Rubinstein, and D. B. da Silva (political dissidents, sacrifice Alex)
P.R. Deltoid (post-correction adviser)


ultraviolence
classical music
choice
brutal state/police brutality
youth
growing up
opposition to the older generation


What kind of a future is depicted? How far away is it?
Nadsat, as opposed to Newspeak?


What kind of a society do you see here? In what way is the novel a child of its time?
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Burgess Clockwork Orange/CheatSheet Clockwork Orange.md

item.title: CheatSheet Hunger Games
item.description:
#YA #dystopian

Suzanne Collins, The Hunger Games,
2008
genre: #YA #dystopian


After Rue’s death. themes: compassion, care; rebellion, growing defiance

KatnissPeeta Mellark(baker's son; boy tribute from District 12)Haymitch Abernathy(former Hunger Games winner; cynical drunk)Gale Hawthorne(Katniss's childhood friend)Effie Trinket(escort from the Capitol assigned to District 12)Prim Everdeen(Katniss's little sister; original District 12 tribute)Rue(girl tribute from District 11)Cinna(stylist from the Capitol)Katniss Everdeen(narrator and heroine; girl tribute from District 12) coachescoachesskeptical of his strategy to win the Games is in love withconfused about her feelings formakes fun ofmakes fun ofHelps her win the crowds over by styling her to look innocentvolunteers to be in the Hunger Games so she doesn't have toformed an alliance withdecorates grave with flowers during Games Recompile


dictatorship
classism
rebellion
rich vs. poor / societal inequality
appearance / looks and popularity
entertainment vs. real suffering and death
compassion, companionship, love vs. mock-emotional displays on TV


bread (Panem)
Mockingjay

item.title: Extract 1 - Hunger Games

item.title: CheatSheet Study in Scarlet
item.description:
#crime

Arthur Conan Doyle, A Study in Scarlet
1st Sherlock Holmes novel, 1887
genre: #crime


Beginning, chapter 2; Watson describes Holmes and his attitude towards knowledge

characterisation of Holmes
practical use of knowledge; what is knowledge good for, anyway?
How do the 2 men compliment each other as a team? Is this already visible from this passage?
How does the plot evolve from Watsons’ meeting Holmes onward?


With this small novel Doyle introduced the world to Sherlock Holmes. The series became a large success. Can you explain why?
The character of Watson is said to be based on Doyle himself. What do they have in common? (naval doctor)
The case they must solve takes its beginning in the States in a Mormon community. How relevant is this?
Comment on the structure of the text. (Large parts of it are recollections of life in the Mormon community in the US; so rather unusual as the crime story actually takes a back seat.
Crime stories – and this is one of the early ones in the genre – help cast an eye on social conditions and social problems not so often brought up in other literature. How can this be seen in this novel?
To what extent are the characters and their peculiarities introduced and detailed in this first Sherlock Holmes novel?

(diagram as picture:)
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Doyle Study in Scarlet/CheatSheet Study in Scarlet.md

item.title: Extract 1 - A Study in Scarlet
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Doyle Study in Scarlet/Extract 1 - A Study in Scarlet.md

item.title: CheatSheet The Great Gatsby
item.description:
#20thcentury

F. Scott Fitzgerald, The Great Gatsby (1925)
genre: #20thcentury


at Gatsby’s party, Nick meets him for the first time

towards the ending of the novel. Gatsby is dead bc. he was shot by cheated husband of Tom’s lover...


wealth
decadence
What role does organised crime play in the novel?
How does Fitzgerald use parties as a tool of commentary on society?
What role does sexuality play?
Has Gatsby been corrupted (by society?)
How morally upright is Nick?


green light
cars
swimming pool
drinks/alcohol
party


New York
“West Egg”
Valley of Ashes
Houses, estates
gas station/garage


How reliable is Nick as a narrator?
What is possible for Fitzpatrick to show and tell which wouldn’t be possible in a third-person narrative?
Analyse the structure of the narrative.
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Fitzgerald The Great Gatsby/CheatSheet The Great Gatsby.md

item.title: CheatSheet Midnight Library
item.description:
#magicrealism

Matt Haig, The Midnight Library (2020)
genre: #magicrealism




How do
regrets
life decisions
the multiverse
endless possibilities


are Nora’s problems, her depression, somehow solved, magically or otherwise? (no! Haig knows exactly that it takes time and effort to get to a better place!)



books
the librarian

item.description:
#sciencefiction

Frank Herbert, Dune, 1965
genre: #sciencefiction

p. 170-171, very end of first chapter, "Dune"


ecology
religion
being chosen


if it’s about ecology... where is this ecology?
if it’s science fiction, why are there only humans?
why all the arab/muslim references? do we have a problem with orientalism, here?

Picture file (svg):

item.title: Extract 1 - Dune

item.title: CheatSheet - Brave New World
item.description:
#dystopian

Aldous Huxley, Brave New World, 1932
genre: #dystopian


Towards the end, the Savage is questioned and instructed by Mustapha Mond

What elements of the world constructed in Brave New World do we see in this passage? (everything guided, hardly any emotions, everybody is supposed to be happy)
Explain the title!
How is Huxley criticising his own “modern” world?
Why does the Savage want inconveniences? Do you find his ideas convincing? Everybody is happy, after all…
What does his wish lead to?
Does this make him a tragic hero? In what way?



How is this book a “child of its time” and how does it transcend time and place?
Why are births and mothers considered the greatest obscenity?
What point is Huxley making about human nature and the nature of human communities?


The Use of Technology to Control Society
The Dangers of an All-Powerful State
The Consumer Society
The Incompatibility of Happiness and Truth
Individuality
Power
Freedom and Confinement
Spirituality


Can you explain the title?
Role of women? Mothers?
Discuss Lenina
Compare and contrast Bernhard and John
Compare the World State to present societies


John: The son of the Director and Linda, grew up outside of the World State, spent his life al-ienated from his village on the New Mexico Savage Reservation, unable to fit in to World State society, entire worldview is based on his knowledge of Shakespeare’s plays.
Bernard Marx: An Alpha male who fails to fit in because of his inferior physical stature, holds unorthodox beliefs about sexual relationships, sports, and community events, insecure about his size and status, discontented with the World State, when threatened Bernard can be petty and cruel
Helmholtz Watson: An Alpha lecturer at the College of Emotional Engineering, prime example of his caste, but feels that his work is empty and meaningless and would like to use his writing abilities for something more meaningful. He and Bernard are friends because they find common ground in their discontent with the World State, but often finds Bernard’s boastfulness and cowardice tedious.
Lenina Crowne: A vaccination worker at the Central London Hatchery and Conditioning Centre, object of desire for a number of major and minor characters, including Bernard Marx and John, she is attracted to Bernard—the misfit—and she develops a violent passion for John the Savage. Ultimately, her values are those of a conventional World State citizen: her primary means of relating to other people is through sex, and she is unable to share Bernard’s disaffection or to comprehend John’s alternate system of values.
Mustapha Mond: The Resident World Controller of Western Europe, one of only ten World Con-trollers, once an ambitious, young scientist performing illicit research, given the choice of going into exile or training to become a World Controller, chose to give up science, and now he cen-sors scientific discoveries and exiles people for unorthodox beliefs.
Fanny Crowne: Lenina Crowne’s friend, role is mainly to voice the conventional values of her caste and society.
Henry Foster: One of Lenina’s many lovers, perfectly conventional Alpha male, casually discuss-ing Lenina’s body with his coworkers. His success with Lenina, and his casual attitude about it, infuriate the jealous Bernard.
Linda: John’s mother, and a Beta. While visiting the New Mexico Savage Reservation, she be-came pregnant with the Director’s son, got lost, suffered a head injury and was left behind, could not get an abortion on the Reservation, and she was too ashamed to return to the World State with a baby, makes her a social outcast, desperate to return to the World State and to soma.
The Director: The Director administrates the Central London Hatchery and Conditioning Centre. He is a threatening figure, with the power to exile Bernard to Iceland, secretly vulnerable be-cause he fathered a child, a scandalous and obscene act in the World State.
Popé: Popé was Linda’s lover on the New Mexico Savage Reservation. He gave Linda a copy of The Complete Works of Shakespeare.
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Huxley Brave New World/CheatSheet - Brave New World.md

item.title: Extract 1 - Brave New World
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Huxley Brave New World/Extract 1 - Brave New World.md

item.title: Extract 2 - Brave New World
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Huxley Brave New World/Extract 2 - Brave New World.md

item.title: CheatSheet The Monk
item.description:
#gothic #romanticism

Matthew Lewis, The Monk (1796)
genre: #gothic #romanticism
Ambrosio falls in love with Matilda, a young girl who has dressed up as a novice monk in order to be close to Ambrosio bc. she has fallen in love with him.
Later, Ambrosio call the devil to help him sleep with Antonia (a noblewoman). He is about to do that but is suprised by Elvira (where Antonia lives) and kills Elvira.
Later he poisons her in order to have her assumedly dead body brought to the crypt, then he goes there when seh’s supposed to wake up. He attempts to rape her, she tries to escape, he stabs her. He’s caught, however, and brought to the inquisition. Matilda (who has helped him all along and is really a demon who was supposed to bring his soul to Lucifer) convinces him to sell his soul to the devil. he does. truths come out (about Matilda), but also about the fact that Antonia was his sister, o it’s incest, too. Hurray!
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Lewis The Monk/CheatSheet The Monk.md

item.title: CheatSheet The Road
item.description:
#dystopian

Cormac McCarthy, The Road, 2006
genre: #dystopian
extract p. 137-139
(hard to place in novel; repetitive, several incidents with towns and houses)
Middle of the novel.
After: cellar with supplies, ship, the man dies, boy picked up by father of a family to live with.
Before seen a little boy whom they didn’t take with them


One of the many houses they search → why?
What happened to Earth?
Warning sign: hint on society
Memories of before catastrophe: why important?
2 people in a mirror: symbolism?
Atmosphere
pictures, scenery, sounds?
what part does the language play in creation of the atmosphere?
Characters: Who’s there in the novel?
Why no names?
Who are the antagonists?
What are the relationships characters have with each other? Describe and analyse them
Science Fiction? Criticism? Message?
Is there hope?
Dystopian: what is the threat in the novel? How does it compare to the other two novels? In what way is “The Road” (2006) a novel of its time?
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/McCarthy The Road/CheatSheet The Road.md

item.title: Extract 1 - The Road
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/McCarthy The Road/Extract 1 - The Road.md

item.title: CheatSheet Circe
item.description:
#classics #feminist

Madeline Miller, Circe, 2018
Genre: #classics #feminist

Extract 1 (1/3-middle part), on Crete. Circe has helped Pasiphaë give birth to the Minotaur
Relationships of the sisters? (not very good... Pasiphaë is manipulative and hungry for power, careless about any kind of relationships)


god vs. mortal
outcast because different
magic
being a woman / change of perspective

Madeline Miller, 2018 Relationship Types:
Gold solid: Divine family bonds
Red dashed: Conflict/antagonism
Pink dashed: Romance/love
Green solid: Friendship/alliance
Blue dashed: Mentorship/guidance
Purple solid: Transformation magic
Orange dashed: Rivalry/competition Circe(Protagonist)Witch, exile, seeker of agencyCompassionate but fierceHelios(Sun God)Circe's fatherProud, distant, traditionalPerse(Oceanid)Circe's motherVain, cold, status-obsessedAeetes(King of Colchis)Circe's brotherCruel, power-hungryPasiphaë(Queen of Crete)Circe's sisterManipulative, ambitiousPersesCirce's brotherWeak, jealous, treacherousGlaucus(Sea God)Circe's first loveNaive, transformed by powerScyllaBeautiful nymphVictim of Circe's jealousyOdysseus(King of Ithaca)Clever, complex, war-wearyCirce's great lovePenelopeOdysseus's wifeLoyal, intelligent, strongTelegonusCirce & Odysseus's sonBrave, kind, mortalAthena(Goddess of Wisdom)Circe's enemyProud, strategic, unforgivingHermes(Messenger God)Trickster, informantPlayful but unreliableDaedalus(Master Craftsman)Brilliant inventorKindred spirit to Circe fathermother fatherfatherfathermotherfather first love true love married enemies sibling rivalrysibling conflictthreatens/betrays transforms to godtransforms to monster friendship/understandingeventual friendship brings news/guidance chooses over Circejealousy/regretdisappoints/exiles dismissive/cold Recompile


How does Circe reflect on the differences between mortals and gods? In what ways are men more threatening than the gods? With whom does she feel more at ease?
Which of the women in Circe’s life have the most impact on her life and choices? Who does she identify with the most and why?
How does Circe’s witchcraft give her strength? How does she feel about the traditional expectation of feminine fragility?
How does Circe change from the beginning of the book to the end? Why do you think she chooses her ultimate path?
p. 325: “We are not our blood.” A repetition from an earlier exchange with Telemachus. How does the novel reflect this thought?
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Miller Circe/CheatSheet Circe.md

item.title: Extract 1 - Circe
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Miller Circe/Extract 1 - Circe.md

item.title: CheatSheet Crucible
item.description:
#play #historicalfiction

Arthur Miller, The Crucible, 1952
genre: #play #historicalfiction
4 Acts

Extract 1 - Crucible: Act 3 (of 4)
Mary Warren is being interviewed and hard pressed
theme: social pressure, peer pressure, saying and doing what you are expected to...

Reverend Parris(minister in Salem)Reverend Hale(minister summoned tocheck for witchcraft)Deputy GovernorDanforth(Judge)John Proctor(Salem farmer)Betty Parris(gravely ill after nightin the woods)Tituba(conjures spirits withgirls in the woods)Thomas Putnam(Salem landowner)Goody Putnam(lost seven children)Ruth Putnam(gravely ill afternight in thewoods)Abigail Williams(leader of girls in the woods)Mercy Lewis(one of the girls in thewoods)Susanna Walcott(one of the girls inthe woods)Mary Warren(one of the girls inthe woods)Sarah Good(beggar in Salem; firstto be accused)Elizabeth Proctor(given doll with needle byMary Warren)Rebecca Nurse(well-respected wife oflandowner Francis Nurse)Giles Corey(husband of womanaccused of witchcraft) father servant toaccusesaccusesthreatens and orders to liethreatens and orders to liethreatens and orders to liethreatens and orders to lieformer loversmarriedfatheronly surviving childaccuses of murdermarriedaccusestries to saveaccused of putting needle byorders pressed to deathunder questioning Recompile


witchcraft and magic are never questioned
witch hunt cycle
accusations
torture / social pressure
confessions
further accusations
political background 1950ies McCartyism
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Miller The Crucible/CheatSheet Crucible.md

item.title: Extract 1 - Crucible
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Miller The Crucible/Extract 1 - Crucible.md

item.title: CheatSheet 1984
item.description:
#dystopian

George Orwell, Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949)
genre: #dystopian

Extract 1: Towards the end, Room 101
Towards end, when Winston is imprisoned and questioned by O’Brien.
Specifically: a photograph which doesn’t officially exist, which has officially never exist-ed is suddenly shown to Winston. He has seen it before, by accident.

Analyse O’Brien and Winston, starting from this passage
How is their relationship?
What is Winston’s relationship to Julia, and how does it develop?
Extract 2:
Beginning of the novel (p. 26-27)
intense worldbuilding
characterisation of Winston
many things seem "wrong":

clock strikes 14
25cent-piece, etc.
Big Brother
slogans


Describe the characters! (protagonist, antagonist)
Why is Winston imprisoned?
Describe the world in 1984
Who has power in the state, Oceania?
How and why does the Party establish and maintain power?
Surveillance
What is the outlook on technology Orwell casts here?
What is doublethink?
Two Minutes Hate
What does the party do to the language, and why?
Do you think this is a valuable means of controlling people?
Changing history: is this legitimate? Would it work? Don’t we do it by in-terpreting history anew for each generation?
Creating an enemy, maintaining external threat
Why: only interested in power, nothing else (explicitly stated)
Who is Big Brother?
What is the background for Orwell to write this novel?
What’s the relevance of the novel today? (surveillance, totalitarian states?)
What do you make of the TV show of the same name: Isn’t that a little bit scary?

Big Brother(leader of the Party)O'Brien(the personification ofthe Party)Emmanuel Goldstein(leader of the Brotherhood, ananti-Party organization)Winston Smith(protagonist who is staunchlyagainst the Party)Julia(Winston's ally who isagainst the Party's doctrines)Parsons(Winston's neighbor)Syme(Newspeak expert)Mr. Charrington(a member of theThought Police)Ampleforth(a poet of sorts who workswith Winston) is an agent pretends to bea follower ofbetraysbetrays arrests vaporizes loves and ultimatelybetrays rooms atrooms at Recompile


What is the background for Orwell to write this novel?
Compared to The Road: nature of “the threat” / child of its time?
What’s the relevance of the novel today? (surveillance, totalitarian states?)
What do you make of the TV show of the same name: Isn’t that a little bit scary?
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Orwell 1984/CheatSheet 1984.md

item.title: Extract 1 – 1984
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Orwell 1984/Extract 1 – 1984.md

item.title: Extract 2 – 1984
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Orwell 1984/Extract 2 – 1984.md

item.title: CheatSheet Animal Farm
item.description:
#dystopian

George Orwell, Animal Farm, 1945
Genre: #dystopian

• Looks and feels a bit like a children's story: is it? why/why not?
• Why would Orwell choose to make this look like a children's story'
• Why choose animals instead of a community of people in order to illustrate what Orwell wants to illustrate?
• What are the real-world backgrounds for Orwell's satire?


communism
egalitarianism (All animals are equal, but some animals are more equal)
ideals getting instrumented and then boilde down to pragmatism, despotism and eventually being forgotten
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Orwell Animal Farm/CheatSheet Animal Farm.md

item.title: CheatSheet The Bell Jar
item.description:
Sylvia Plath, The Bell Jar, 1963
set in the mid-50ies
roman a clef, closely autobiographic


towards the end of the novel, when Esther wants a diaphragma fitted
freedom, but illegal; injustice btw. men and women, etc.


lack of orientation
mental illness
women and social expectations
suicide
place of oneself, distance from the world
prospects, not least as a young woman of the 1950ies
ambitions


What is expected from Esther?
What does Esther want from life?
Why the title?

Esther GreenwoodMrsBuddy WillardConstantinMarcoIrwinDoreenBetsyJoan GillingDrDr GordonDr NolanMrs WillardPhilomena GuineaGreenwoodGordonNolan motherformer boyfriendbrief romantic interestassault incidentaffairfriend/roommate in NYCfriend in NYCacquaintance/fellow patientfirst psychiatristsecond psychiatrist (positive)former girlfriendmotherbenefactor Recompile

Light blue: Main character and supportive figures
Light green: Family members
Light coral/Red: Romantic interests (red for Marco due to the assault)
Yellow: Friends from New York
Orange: Fellow psychiatric patients
Light gray: Unsympathetic medical professionals
Gold: Mentor/benefactor figures
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Plath The Bell Jar/CheatSheet The Bell Jar.md

item.title: Extract 1 - The Bell Jar

item.title: CheatSheet Maus
item.description:
Art Spiegelmann, The Complete Maus, 1996


Holocaust
survivor’s story


framework plot / frame story
graphic novel → effect? what can Spiegelman tell differently through a graphic novel?


what do you make of the fact that "peoples" are distinguished via an animal species?
racism?
masks to hide
why, at the beginning of the 2nd book, are Artie and Françoise wearing mouse masks, you think?
D2 Compilation Error:The operation was abortedEsther GreenwoodMrsBuddy WillardConstantinMarcoIrwinDoreenBetsyJoan GillingDrDr GordonDr NolanMrs WillardPhilomena GuineaGreenwoodGordonNolan motherformer boyfriendbrief romantic interestassault incidentaffairfriend/roommate in NYCfriend in NYCacquaintance/fellow patientfirst psychiatristsecond psychiatrist (positive)former girlfriendmotherbenefactor




Blue: Present Day Characters
Red: Holocaust Era/Past Characters
Green: Extended Family & Friends


Orange: Parent-child relationships
Blue: Documentation/interview relationships
Pink: Marriage/current romantic relationships
Deep Pink: Profound love (Vladek-Anja)
Light Pink: Former romantic relationships
Purple: Therapy/haunting memories
Light Green: Family bonds
Dark Green: Survival partnerships/prisoner friendships
Bright Green: Rescuers/helpers during Holocaust
Plum: In-law relationships
Red: Violence/persecution/death
Light Orange: Complicated family relationships
item.guid: 01 English/Literature/Matura books/Spiegelman Maus/CheatSheet Maus.md
